---
title: "Session 6: AI Foundations"
subtitle: "How LLMs and AI coding assistants work, and how to use them in research"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## This session is under development
Content for this session will be added before **April 27, 2026**. The outline below reflects the planned structure. Version control is covered in [Sessions 4–5](../version-control/session4.qmd).
:::

## Planned content

### The AI landscape for researchers (~15 min) {#the-ai-landscape-for-researchers}

- **Chat interfaces:** Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini. Who makes what.
- **Coding assistants:** Claude Code (Anthropic), GitHub Copilot (GitHub / Microsoft; uses multiple backend models), Cursor (Anysphere; an IDE that wraps Claude, GPT, Gemini), Codex (OpenAI).
- **Models vs. products.** Some of these are models; others are products that route to models. The practical implication is that when you document AI use for replication, you record both.
- **How LLMs work at a high level.** Tokens, context windows, model families, the gap between training data and current reality.

### AI for coding: live demo (~25 min) {#ai-for-coding-live-demo}

- Write R code for a realistic data task with an LLM.
- The workflow: prompt, review, iterate, verify.
- Common failure modes: hallucinated functions, wrong package syntax, subtle statistical errors.
- How version control (Session 4–5) is the safety net: every AI change is a commit you can review and revert.

### Best practices for AI-assisted research code (~15 min) {#best-practices}

- Verify every output before committing.
- Use AI as a first draft, not a final answer.
- Prompt engineering basics: be specific, provide context, ask for explanations.
- Treat AI suggestions with the same skepticism you would apply to a well-meaning but unfamiliar RA.

### Hands-on exercise (~15 min) {#hands-on-exercise}

- Students use an LLM to write an R script for a simple task.
- Compare outputs across tools.
- Commit the result to their Git repo.

### Wrap-up (~5 min) {#wrap-up}

- Preview of Session 7: journal disclosure policies and ethical scenarios.
